did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses 2D and 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), which are deep learning models, to classify skin melanoma. The study experimentally compares these AI models on dermatology datasets (PH2, ISIC Archive, and ISIC Skin Cancer) to evaluate their performance in identifying melanoma and other skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is an original experimental study presented at the 2024 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Emerging Communication Technologies (ICEC). The abstract explicitly states that the authors 'experimentally compared 2D and 3D Convolution Neural Network (CNN) models' using specific datasets (PH2, ISIC Archive, ISIC Skin Cancer) to determine accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 scores. It presents new results and conclusions regarding the performance of these models, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Comparative Study of 2D and 3D Convolutional Neural Networks for Melanoma Classification
journal: 2024 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Emerging Communication Technologies (ICEC)
brief_summary: This study experimentally compared 2D and 3D Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for classifying skin melanoma using the PH2, ISIC Archive, and ISIC Skin Cancer datasets. The authors evaluated model performance using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and ROC curves after splitting the data into training and testing sets. The results indicated that the 2D CNN model demonstrated enhanced capabilities in detecting skin lesion structures and achieved higher classification accuracy than the 3D CNN. Although 3D CNNs are computationally efficient, they did not improve classification accuracy by analyzing volumetric data in this context. The study concludes that 2D CNNs are more effective for this specific task, while noting that both models showed lower accuracies than ideal primary techniques, suggesting a need for further research.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target for classification in the PH2 dataset (as one of three classes) and the ISIC Archive dataset (as the malignant class). The study focuses on identifying melanoma using 2D and 3D CNNs.

all_task_types.target: Skin melanoma diagnosis (Malignant vs Benign) and skin lesion categorization (3-class and 9-class)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the objective is 'melanoma classification' and 'identifying melanoma'. It evaluates models on three datasets with discrete class labels: PH2 (3 classes: common nevi, atypical nevi, melanomas), ISIC Archive (2 classes: benign, malignant), and ISIC Skin Cancer (9 classes: actinic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevus, pigmented benign keratosis, seborrheic keratosis, squamous cell carcinoma, vascular lesion). Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, and ROC curves, which are standard metrics for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: PH2 dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database for research and benchmarking, containing RGB color images of skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin tumors, analyzed in this study for binary classification.

datasets_used.name: ISIC skin cancer dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset available on Kaggle containing nine different tumor types, used for multi-class classification.

use.other: The dataset was already split and available on Kaggle.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between 2D and 3D CNNs were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score) and visual inspection of ROC curves and confusion matrices.

PH2 dataset.notes: The 2D CNN model showed enhanced capabilities to detect skin lesion structures and achieved higher classification accuracy than the 3D CNN. The dataset contains three classes: common nevi, atypical nevi, and melanomas.

ISIC Archive dataset.notes: The dataset contains two classes: benign and malignant. The 2D CNN model demonstrated better performance across metrics compared to the 3D CNN.

ISIC Skin Cancer dataset.notes: The dataset contains nine tumor types. The 2D CNN model outperformed the 3D CNN in all reported metrics. The paper notes that both models showed lower accuracies than ideal primary techniques.

architecture_used.pretraining: None (models were built specifically for the task)

classification_or_output_head.units: Variable (depends on dataset: 3 for PH2, 2 for ISIC Archive, 9 for ISIC Skin Cancer)

preprocessing_techniques: Segmentation: Finding the area of interest using thresholding methods (finding maximum values for skin & lesion areas and choosing a middle threshold) and merging/region-growing methods such as statistical region merging.
Image Resizing: Resizing images to a fixed size of 128x128 pixels to ensure consistent input dimensions for the CNN models.
conclusions: The 2D CNN model demonstrated enhanced capabilities to detect skin lesion structures compared to the 3D CNN.
The classification accuracy of the 2D CNN was found to be better than that of the 3D CNN across the tested datasets (PH2, ISIC Archive, and ISIC Skin Cancer).
The 3D CNN models did not improve classification accuracy by analyzing volumetric data in this context, despite being computationally efficient.
Both 2D and 3D CNN models showed lower classification accuracies than ideal primary techniques for skin melanoma classification.
Further study is required before these models can be widely used in clinical practice due to their current diagnostic accuracy levels.
important_remarks: The study relies on custom-built 2D and 3D CNN architectures rather than established, pre-trained models, which limits the generalizability of the findings compared to state-of-the-art benchmarks.

Classification performance was notably low across all datasets (e.g., 2D CNN accuracy of 0.37 on ISIC Archive and 0.35 on ISIC Skin Cancer), leading the authors to conclude that the models are not yet suitable as primary diagnostic techniques.

The 3D CNN was applied to standard 2D dermoscopic images by replicating channels to create a 3D input (5x128x128x3), which is methodologically questionable for static images and may not reflect true volumetric analysis.
The PH2 dataset is very small (200 images total), raising concerns about statistical power and the risk of overfitting, despite the 80/20 split.

The ISIC Skin Cancer dataset used was sourced from Kaggle with a pre-existing split (2239 training, 118 testing), which may not represent the full distribution of the original ISIC Archive and introduces potential data leakage or selection bias risks.
No external validation was performed; the models were only tested on the specific splits of the three public datasets used for training.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) to validate the observed performance differences between 2D and 3D CNNs.
Data augmentation techniques were not employed, which is a significant limitation for deep learning models trained on relatively small medical imaging datasets.
The preprocessing involved simple thresholding and region-growing for segmentation, which may be less robust than modern automated segmentation methods.
